revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|prefecture={{ill|Okaya|WD=Q838672}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.035194|N|138.068639|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11630661]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11630661]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kannoni-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = {{image requested|cat=岡谷市}} | location = Minato 4-chome 15-22, Okaya, Nagano Prefecture, Japan | latitude_deg = 36 | latitude_min = 2 | latitude_sec = 6.7 | longitude_deg = 138 | longitude_min = 4 | longitude_sec = 7.1 | sect = [[:en:Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] | deity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara]] | founded = | founder = | full_name = Ryūkōzan Kannoni-in | former_affiliation = [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] (Shabō) | noteworthy = Historical ties to {{ill|Suwa Goryōnin|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Goryōnin|1=Suwa Goryōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}} | map = Nagano Prefecture | map_relief = yes | map_width = 256 }} '''Kannoni-in''' (観音院), also known as '''Kosaka Kannoni-in''', is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] sect located in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya]], Nagano Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Ryūkōzan''' (龍光山), and its principal object of worship is the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara]].
== History == Originally a {{ill|Shabō|ja|社坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神宮寺|1=jingū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1074971}} (a temple associated with a Shinto shrine) of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], Kannoni-in served as a {{ill|Prayer Temple|ja|祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigan-sho|qq=}} for the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]] during the medieval period. The temple is situated on a hill overlooking [[:en:Lake Suwa|Lake Suwa]], providing a scenic view of the surrounding area.
The temple was revived during the early Edo period under the patronage of {{ill|Suwa Tadatane|ja|諏訪忠恒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatane|1=Suwa Tadatsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631876}}, the lord of the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by {{ill|Suwa Tadatora|ja|諏訪忠虎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatora|1=Suwa Tadatora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631882}}.
The temple is featured in [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goryōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. The 2007 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga Drama]] adaptation of *Fūrin Kazan* also portrays the temple.
In 1963 (Shōwa 38), a memorial tower dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin was erected on the temple grounds.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Main Hall''' - Rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by Suwa Tadatora. * '''Memorial Tower''' - Dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin in 1963.
== References == * ''Shinshu Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'' (1981). Shinano Mainichi Shimbun. * ''Exploring Shinshu's Ancient Temples: Tendai and Shingon Sects'' (1996). Kyodo Publishing.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kosaka Castle|ja|小坂城 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosaka Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Minato Village|ja|湊村 (長野県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Village|1=Minato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563162}} * {{ill|Okaya City Landmarks|ja|岡谷市#名所・旧跡・観光スポット|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okaya City Landmarks|1=Okaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838672}}

The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Ryūkōzan''' (龍光山), and its principal object of worship is the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara]].

The temple is featured in [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goryōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. The 2007 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga Drama]] adaptation of *Fūrin Kazan* also portrays the temple.
In 1963 (Shōwa 38), a memorial tower dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin was erected on the temple grounds.

The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Ryūkōzan''' (龍光山), and its principal object of worship is the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara]].
== History == Originally a {{ill|Shabō|ja|社坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神宮寺|1=jingū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1074971}} (a temple associated with a Shinto shrine) of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], Kannoni-in served as a {{ill|Prayer Temple|ja|祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigan-sho|qq=}} for the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]] during the medieval period. The temple is situated on a hill overlooking [[:en:Lake Suwa|Lake Suwa]], providing a scenic view of the surrounding area.
The temple was revived during the early Edo period under the patronage of {{ill|Suwa Tadatane|ja|諏訪忠恒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatane|1=Suwa Tadatsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631876}}, the lord of the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by {{ill|Suwa Tadatora|ja|諏訪忠虎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatora|1=Suwa Tadatora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631882}}.
The temple is featured in [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goryōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. The 2007 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga Drama]] adaptation of *Fūrin Kazan* also portrays the temple.
In 1963 (Shōwa 38), a memorial tower dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin was erected on the temple grounds.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Main Hall''' - Rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by Suwa Tadatora. * '''Memorial Tower''' - Dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin in 1963.
== References == * ''Shinshu Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'' (1981). Shinano Mainichi Shimbun. * ''Exploring Shinshu's Ancient Temples: Tendai and Shingon Sects'' (1996). Kyodo Publishing.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kosaka Castle|ja|小坂城 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosaka Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Minato Village|ja|湊村 (長野県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Village|1=Minato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563162}} * {{ill|Okaya City Landmarks|ja|岡谷市#名所・旧跡・観光スポット|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okaya City Landmarks|1=Okaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838672}}
== External Links == * [http://www.kanko-okaya.jp/tabitabi/jsp/furin/ Okaya City Tourism - Kosaka Kannoni-in]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|prefecture={{ill|Okaya|WD=Q838672}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.035194|N|138.068639|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11630661]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11630661]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kannoni-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = {{image requested|cat=岡谷市}} | location = Minato 4-chome 15-22, Okaya, Nagano Prefecture, Japan | latitude_deg = 36 | latitude_min = 2 | latitude_sec = 6.7 | longitude_deg = 138 | longitude_min = 4 | longitude_sec = 7.1 | sect = [[:en:Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] | deity = [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara]] | founded = | founder = | full_name = Ryūkōzan Kannoni-in | former_affiliation = [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] (Shabō) | noteworthy = Historical ties to {{ill|Suwa Goryōnin|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Goryōnin|1=Suwa Goryōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}} | map = Nagano Prefecture | map_relief = yes | map_width = 256 }} '''Kannoni-in''' (観音院), also known as '''Kosaka Kannoni-in''', is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha]] sect located in [[:en:Okaya, Nagano|Okaya]], Nagano Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Ryūkōzan''' (龍光山), and its principal object of worship is the [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara]].
== History == Originally a {{ill|Shabō|ja|社坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神宮寺|1=jingū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1074971}} (a temple associated with a Shinto shrine) of [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]], Kannoni-in served as a {{ill|Prayer Temple|ja|祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigan-sho|qq=}} for the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]] during the medieval period. The temple is situated on a hill overlooking [[:en:Lake Suwa|Lake Suwa]], providing a scenic view of the surrounding area.
The temple was revived during the early Edo period under the patronage of {{ill|Suwa Tadatane|ja|諏訪忠恒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatane|1=Suwa Tadatsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631876}}, the lord of the [[:en:Suwa Domain|Suwa Domain]]. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by {{ill|Suwa Tadatora|ja|諏訪忠虎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatora|1=Suwa Tadatora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631882}}.
The temple is featured in [[:en:Yasushi Inoue|Yasushi Inoue]]'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goryōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]]. The 2007 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga Drama]] adaptation of *Fūrin Kazan* also portrays the temple.
In 1963 (Shōwa 38), a memorial tower dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin was erected on the temple grounds.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Main Hall''' - Rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by Suwa Tadatora. * '''Memorial Tower''' - Dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin in 1963.
== References == * ''Shinshu Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'' (1981). Shinano Mainichi Shimbun. * ''Exploring Shinshu's Ancient Temples: Tendai and Shingon Sects'' (1996). Kyodo Publishing.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kosaka Castle|ja|小坂城 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosaka Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Minato Village|ja|湊村 (長野県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Village|1=Minato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563162}} * {{ill|Okaya City Landmarks|ja|岡谷市#名所・旧跡・観光スポット|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okaya City Landmarks|1=Okaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838672}}
== External Links == * [http://www.kanko-okaya.jp/tabitabi/jsp/furin/ Okaya City Tourism - Kosaka Kannoni-in]

== History == Originally a {{ill|Shabō|ja|社坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神宮寺|1=jingū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1074971}} (a temple associated with a Shinto shrine) of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}}, Kannoni-in served as a {{ill|Prayer Temple|ja|祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigan-sho|qq=}} for the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan}} during the medieval period. The temple is situated on a hill overlooking {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|WD=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa}}, providing a scenic view of the surrounding area.
The temple was revived during the early Edo period under the patronage of {{ill|Suwa Tadatane|ja|諏訪忠恒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatane|1=Suwa Tadatsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631876}}, the lord of the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|WD=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain}}. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by {{ill|Suwa Tadatora|ja|諏訪忠虎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatora|1=Suwa Tadatora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631882}}.
The temple is featured in {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue}}'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goryōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori}}. The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Ryūkōzan''' (龍光山), and its principal object of worship is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara}}.
== History == Originally a {{ill|Shabō|ja|社坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神宮寺|1=jingū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1074971}} (a temple associated with a Shinto shrine) of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha}}, Kannoni-in served as a {{ill|Prayer Temple|ja|祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigan-sho|qq=}} for the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan}} during the medieval period. The temple is situated on a hill overlooking {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|WD=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa}}, providing a scenic view of the surrounding area.
The temple was revived during the early Edo period under the patronage of {{ill|Suwa Tadatane|ja|諏訪忠恒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatane|1=Suwa Tadatsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631876}}, the lord of the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|WD=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain}}. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by {{ill|Suwa Tadatora|ja|諏訪忠虎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatora|1=Suwa Tadatora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631882}}.
The temple is featured in {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue}}'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goryōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori}}. The 2007 NHK {{ill|Taiga drama|en|Taiga drama|WD=Q1200935|lt=Taiga Drama}} adaptation of *Fūrin Kazan* also portrays the temple.
In 1963 (Shōwa 38), a memorial tower dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin was erected on the temple grounds.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Main Hall''' - Rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by Suwa Tadatora. * '''Memorial Tower''' - Dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin in 1963.
== References == * ''Shinshu Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'' (1981). Shinano Mainichi Shimbun. * ''Exploring Shinshu's Ancient Temples: Tendai and Shingon Sects'' (1996). Kyodo Publishing.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kosaka Castle|ja|小坂城 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosaka Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Minato Village|ja|湊村 (長野県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Village|1=Minato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563162}} * {{ill|Okaya City Landmarks|ja|岡谷市#名所・旧跡・観光スポット|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okaya City Landmarks|1=Okaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838672}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:観音院 (岡谷市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|prefecture={{ill|Okaya|WD=Q838672|1=Okaya, Nagano}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.035194|N|138.068639|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kannoni-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = {{image requested|cat=岡谷市}} | location = Minato 4-chome 15-22, Okaya, Nagano Prefecture, Japan | latitude_deg = 36 | latitude_min = 2 | latitude_sec = 6.7 | longitude_deg = 138 | longitude_min = 4 | longitude_sec = 7.1 | sect = {{ill|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|en|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|WD=Q674374|lt=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|1=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha}} | deity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara|1=Ekādaśamukha}} | founded = | founder = | full_name = Ryūkōzan Kannoni-in | former_affiliation = {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} (Shabō) | noteworthy = Historical ties to {{ill|Suwa Goryōnin|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Goryōnin|1=Suwa Goronin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}} | map = Nagano Prefecture | map_relief = yes | map_width = 256 }} '''Kannoni-in''' (観音院), also known as '''Kosaka Kannoni-in''', is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|en|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|WD=Q674374|lt=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|1=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha}} sect located in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya|1=Okaya, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Ryūkōzan''' (龍光山), and its principal object of worship is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara|1=Ekādaśamukha}}.
== History == Originally a {{ill|Shabō|ja|社坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神宮寺|1=Jingū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1074971}} (a temple associated with a Shinto shrine) of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}, Kannoni-in served as a {{ill|Prayer Temple|ja|祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigan-sho|qq=}} for the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan|1=Suwa clan}} during the medieval period. The temple is situated on a hill overlooking {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|WD=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa|1=Lake Suwa}}, providing a scenic view of the surrounding area.
The temple was revived during the early Edo period under the patronage of {{ill|Suwa Tadatane|ja|諏訪忠恒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatane|1=Suwa Tadatsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631876}}, the lord of the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|WD=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain|1=Suwa Domain}}. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by {{ill|Suwa Tadatora|ja|諏訪忠虎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatora|1=Suwa Tadatora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631882}}.
The temple is featured in {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue|1=Yasushi Inoue}}'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goronin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}. The 2007 NHK {{ill|Taiga drama|en|Taiga drama|WD=Q1200935|lt=Taiga Drama|1=Taiga drama}} adaptation of *Fūrin Kazan* also portrays the temple.
In 1963 (Shōwa 38), a memorial tower dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin was erected on the temple grounds.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Main Hall''' - Rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by Suwa Tadatora. * '''Memorial Tower''' - Dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin in 1963.
== References == * ''Shinshu Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'' (1981). Shinano Mainichi Shimbun. * ''Exploring Shinshu's Ancient Temples: Tendai and Shingon Sects'' (1996). Kyodo Publishing.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kosaka Castle|ja|小坂城 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosaka Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Minato Village|ja|湊村 (長野県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Village|1=Minato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563162}} * {{ill|Okaya City Landmarks|ja|岡谷市#名所・旧跡・観光スポット|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okaya City Landmarks|1=Okaya, Nagano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838672}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Kannoni-in | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = {{image requested|cat=岡谷市}} | location = Minato 4-chome 15-22, Okaya, Nagano Prefecture, Japan | latitude_deg = 36 | latitude_min = 2 | latitude_sec = 6.7 | longitude_deg = 138 | longitude_min = 4 | longitude_sec = 7.1 | sect = {{ill|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|en|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|WD=Q674374|lt=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|1=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha}} | deity = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara|1=Ekādaśamukha}} | founded = | founder = | full_name = Ryūkōzan Kannoni-in | former_affiliation = {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} (Shabō) | noteworthy = Historical ties to {{ill|Suwa Goryōnin|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Goryōnin|1=Suwa Goronin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}} | map = Nagano Prefecture | map_relief = yes | map_width = 256 }} '''Kannoni-in''' (観音院), also known as '''Kosaka Kannoni-in''', is a Buddhist temple of the {{ill|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|en|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|WD=Q674374|lt=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|1=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha}} sect located in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya|1=Okaya, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture, Japan. The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Ryūkōzan''' (龍光山), and its principal object of worship is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara|1=Ekādaśamukha}}.
== History == Originally a {{ill|Shabō|ja|社坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神宮寺|1=Jingū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1074971}} (a temple associated with a Shinto shrine) of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|WD=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}, Kannoni-in served as a {{ill|Prayer Temple|ja|祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigan-sho|qq=}} for the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan|1=Suwa clan}} during the medieval period. The temple is situated on a hill overlooking {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|WD=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa|1=Lake Suwa}}, providing a scenic view of the surrounding area.
The temple was revived during the early Edo period under the patronage of {{ill|Suwa Tadatane|ja|諏訪忠恒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatane|1=Suwa Tadatsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631876}}, the lord of the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|WD=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain|1=Suwa Domain}}. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by {{ill|Suwa Tadatora|ja|諏訪忠虎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatora|1=Suwa Tadatora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631882}}.
The temple is featured in {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|WD=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue|1=Yasushi Inoue}}'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goronin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}. The 2007 NHK {{ill|Taiga drama|en|Taiga drama|WD=Q1200935|lt=Taiga Drama|1=Taiga drama}} adaptation of *Fūrin Kazan* also portrays the temple.
In 1963 (Shōwa 38), a memorial tower dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin was erected on the temple grounds.
== Cultural Properties == * '''Main Hall''' - Rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by Suwa Tadatora. * '''Memorial Tower''' - Dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin in 1963.
== References == * ''Shinshu Culture Series: Temples and Shrines'' (1981). Shinano Mainichi Shimbun. * ''Exploring Shinshu's Ancient Temples: Tendai and Shingon Sects'' (1996). Kyodo Publishing.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kosaka Castle|ja|小坂城 (岡谷市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosaka Castle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Minato Village|ja|湊村 (長野県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Village|1=Minato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563162}} * {{ill|Okaya City Landmarks|ja|岡谷市#名所・旧跡・観光スポット|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okaya City Landmarks|1=Okaya, Nagano|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q838672}}
== External Links == * [http://www.kanko-okaya.jp/tabitabi/jsp/furin/ Okaya City Tourism - Kosaka Kannoni-in]

The temple's mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Ryūkōzan''' (龍光山), and its principal object of worship is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Avalokitesvara|1=Ekādaśamukha}}.
== History == Originally a {{ill|Shabō|ja|社坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shabō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神宮寺|1=Jingū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1074971}} (a temple associated with a Shinto shrine) of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}}, Kannoni-in served as a {{ill|Prayer Temple|ja|祈願所|1=Kigan-sho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prayer Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kigan-sho|qq=}} for the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|qid=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan|1=Suwa clan}} during the medieval period. The temple is situated on a hill overlooking {{ill|Lake Suwa|en|Lake Suwa|qid=Q1206692|lt=Lake Suwa|1=Lake Suwa}}, providing a scenic view of the surrounding area.
The temple was revived during the early Edo period under the patronage of {{ill|Suwa Tadatane|ja|諏訪忠恒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatane|1=Suwa Tadatsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11631876}}, the lord of the {{ill|Suwa Domain|en|Suwa Domain|qid=Q3034531|lt=Suwa Domain|1=Suwa Domain}}. The current main hall was rebuilt in 1715 (Shōtoku 5) by {{ill|Suwa Tadatora|ja|諏訪忠虎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Tadatora|1=Suwa Tadatora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11631882}}.
The temple is featured in {{ill|Yasushi Inoue|en|Yasushi Inoue|qid=Q712734|lt=Yasushi Inoue|1=Yasushi Inoue}}'s historical novel *{{ill|Fūrin Kazan (novel)|ja|風林火山 (小説)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūrin Kazan (novel)|1=The Samurai Banner of Furin Kazan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11665512}}*, where it serves as the residence of {{ill|Yufu-hime|ja|諏訪御料人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufu-hime|1=Suwa Goronin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193299}}, a concubine of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the mother of {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|qid=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}. In 1963 (Shōwa 38), a memorial tower dedicated to Suwa Goryōnin was erected on the temple grounds.
